Article.ArticleTitle: Multiscale thermodynamic insights into membrane fouling control by biochar-activated peroxymonosulfate pretreatment: Synergy of oxidation and adsorption.

Abstract.AbstractText: Biochar-activated peroxymonosulfate (BC/PMS) pretreatment, combining oxidation and adsorption, has emerged as a promising approach for mitigating membrane fouling. Nevertheless, the fundamental mechanisms underlying this dual-function control of membrane fouling remain poorly understood. In this study, the effects of BC/PMS pretreatment intensity on membrane fouling were examined by integrating experimental analysis, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and thermodynamic modeling. Filtration experiments revealed a strong dependence of fouling behavior on oxidation intensity: low-intensity oxidation aggravated fouling, resulting in a 15-fold increase in specific filtration resistance (SFR), while high-intensity oxidation significantly mitigated fouling, reducing SFR by nearly 145-fold. DFT calculations and chemical characterizations indicated that low-intensity oxidation enhanced foulant-biochar interactions, forming compact adsorption complexes and dense gel layers. In contrast, high-intensity oxidation disrupted the intermolecular associations between foulants and biochar, resulting in a looser, more porous cake layer with enhanced permeability. Interfacial thermodynamic modeling indicated that low-intensity oxidation reduced the hydration radius of foulant-biochar adsorption complexes and lowered the adhesion energy barrier by 75.68 %, while high-intensity oxidation decreased the total adhesion energy by 58.43 %. Furthermore, Flory-Huggins theory revealed that changes in the fouling layer structure and its water-binding capacity, driven by differences in chemical potential, were key determinants of filtration behavior. These findings provide multiscale thermodynamic insights into the intensity-governed synergy of oxidation and adsorption during BC/PMS pretreatment, laying a solid theoretical foundation for the development of effective membrane fouling control technologies.
